comment.body:
the post talks about the long-run.
as vol is mean reverting, being short vol and long theta will always offer better edge than being long vol and short theta.
BS model also created that way. options are never traded at their fair value
comment.raw_body:

user.name: Alma

comment.body:
Thank you, I am very excited for the greeks educational post. I would also love if you went deeper into some of the strategies that you referenced in that post you linked.

- from what I understood, choosing a long or short option position depends on the gamma and vega at play. Given a 20 delta contract, we can assume 20% chance ITM. So as you noted, the short option is the clear winner in isolation. However, when we consider the expected magnitude of the spot and vol, then you can have more input. Generally, it seems clear that when the expectations of spot and vol increase (which generally go together I'm assuming because if you expect a huge range then vol should also increase with it), then you prefer long options. This seems oversimplified though and assumes a lot of great input like predicting range and vol haha
- when you saw good take profit vs. Add to position levels. It's difficult for me to manage when to distinguish between the two still. My instinct always says just take profit but there are numerous times when it would've been better to add on a momentary dip/retest of key level 
- I loved reading some of the specific strategies you suggest like futures + options, which delta options you prefer situationally, when to consider a breakout vs fake out. I would love to see some real examples so I could follow charts

These are just requests from a very appreciative subscriber. I hope I am not coming off demanding, not my intention at all.
Thank you I love the educational content and sharing your knowledge. This is so interesting and helpful; looking forward to more
comment.raw_body:
Thank you, I am very excited for the greeks educational post. I would also love if you went deeper into some of the strategies that you referenced in that post you linked.

- from what I understood, choosing a long or short option position depends on the gamma and vega at play. Given a 20 delta contract, we can assume 20% chance ITM. So as you noted, the short option is the clear winner in isolation. However, when we consider the expected magnitude of the spot and vol, then you can have more input. Generally, it seems clear that when the expectations of spot and vol increase (which generally go together I'm assuming because if you expect a huge range then vol should also increase with it), then you prefer long options. This seems oversimplified though and assumes a lot of great input like predicting range and vol haha
- when you saw good take profit vs. Add to position levels. It's difficult for me to manage when to distinguish between the two still. My instinct always says just take profit but there are numerous times when it would've been better to add on a momentary dip/retest of key level 
- I loved reading some of the specific strategies you suggest like futures + options, which delta options you prefer situationally, when to consider a breakout vs fake out. I would love to see some real examples so I could follow charts

These are just requests from a very appreciative subscriber. I hope I am not coming off demanding, not my intention at all.
Thank you I love the educational content and sharing your knowledge. This is so interesting and helpful; looking forward to more

comment.body:
Alma : Could you kindly clarify your view on this. I see two statements in the weekly post. One states that the risk window open post Opex and especially on Tuesday 22nd. The other one states significant buyback flows will come after post Opex. 

Which one has higher chances and put simply how can the risk window open up post opex when significant buyback is expected ?

"Market is locked up into a range, with downside is well-supported, and this takes me to think that window of risk opens after post-OpEx Tuesday."

"The least charm is around 6511 (assuming constant vol level). And this also means that after the OpEx we can expect significant buyback flow!"

comment.body: I dont think the statements are mutually exclusive. Not sure how to interpret the charm reference though.
